page: Amazon Web Services h1.16xlarge is a storage-optimized server instance featuring 64 vCPUs and 32 physical cores powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor running at 2.3 GHz. The instance utilizes the Xen hypervisor on an x86_64 architecture. It is equipped with 256.0 GB of system memory, yielding 4.0 GB per core, and includes 16,000 GB of local HDD storage. Network connectivity is provisioned with a baseline bandwidth of 25 Gbps. No GPU accelerators are present on this hardware configuration.

Performance benchmarks indicate a clear divergence between single-core and multi-core capabilities. Single-core metrics, including stress-ng single-core CPU performance, Geekbench single-core, and single-threaded gzip compression, fall into the bottom 10% tier. Conversely, multi-core performance is average, with stress-ng multi-core, Geekbench multi-core, and multi-threaded bzip3 decompression placing in the middle 50%. Memory bandwidth is average for both small and large block reads, though the PassMark memory score is weak, placing in the bottom 25%. Notably, PassMark database operations register in the top 25%, indicating strong capability for specific transactional workloads despite the underlying HDD storage medium.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the h1.16xlarge offers a high volume of local HDD storage relative to its compute resources, positioning it as a cost-effective option for sequential data access patterns. However, the reliance on HDD storage instead of SSDs introduces latency tradeoffs for random I/O. The instance is suited for workloads requiring high-capacity local storage and parallelized CPU processing, such as large-scale data warehousing, MapReduce jobs, log processing, and distributed file systems, while being less optimal for single-threaded applications or memory-bound tasks.
description: Amazon Web Services h1.16xlarge is a storage-optimized server instance featuring 64 vCPUs and 32 physical cores powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor at 2.3 GHz. Built on the x86_64 architecture with the Xen hypervisor, it is configured with 256.0 GB of memory and 16,000 GB of local HDD storage, alongside a 25 Gbps baseline network bandwidth. Benchmark data reveals poor single-core CPU performance, placing in the bottom 10%, but average multi-core CPU and memory bandwidth performance. Notably, PassMark database operations perform in the top 25%, demonstrating strong efficiency for database workloads. The instance represents a cost-effective option for high-capacity storage needs, though the HDD storage and weak memory score present latency tradeoffs. It is best suited for distributed file systems, log processing, and data warehousing.
og_description: Amazon Web Services h1.16xlarge is a storage-optimized server featuring 64 vCPUs, 256 GB RAM, and 16,000 GB of local HDD storage, delivering strong database performance with average multi-core compute.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services h1.16xlarge is a storage-optimized server featuring 64 vCPUs, 256 GB of RAM, and 16,000 GB of local HDD storage for data-intensive workloads.
tagline: A storage-optimized instance combining high-capacity local HDD storage with multi-core processing for data-intensive and database workloads.
bullet_points: 64 vCPUs and 32 physical cores powered by Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processors
16,000 GB of local HDD storage for high-capacity sequential data requirements
256.0 GB of system memory and 25 Gbps baseline network bandwidth
Strong PassMark database operations performance, ranking in the top 25%
Poor single-core CPU performance but average multi-core execution capabilities
Suited for distributed file systems, log processing, and data warehousing
